class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Macroeconomics and Measuring the Economy
learning_objectives: Explain the scope of macroeconomics and key macroeconomic questions
Calculate GDP using expenditure and income approaches
Distinguish between nominal and real GDP

lecture_notes.introduction: Macroeconomics studies the economy as a whole: output, employment, inflation, and growth. Understanding these forces is essential for business managers navigating economic cycles.
key_concepts: GDP: market value of all final goods and services produced within a country
Expenditure approach: GDP = C + I + G + NX
Real vs. nominal GDP: adjusting for inflation
GDP deflator and CPI as price level measures
Limitations of GDP as a welfare measure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating GDP from national accounts data and comparing real GDP growth across countries using World Bank data
setup_requirements: Textbook: Mankiw, Principles of Macroeconomics (10th ed.)

review.value: Consumption (~68%), Investment (~18%), Government (~17%), Net Exports (~-3%) for the US; components vary significantly across countries
Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP uses constant base-year prices. Only real GDP growth reflects actual increase in goods and services produced.

learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret unemployment rate, labor force participation, and U-6
Distinguish between frictional, structural, and cyclical unemployment
Analyze the natural rate of unemployment and full employment

lecture_notes.introduction: Employment is the most personally impactful macroeconomic variable. Understanding labor market dynamics helps businesses plan hiring and compensation strategies.
key_concepts: Labor force = employed + unemployed (actively seeking)
Unemployment rate = unemployed / labor force x 100
Types: frictional (job search), structural (skills mismatch), cyclical (recession)
Natural rate of unemployment: frictional + structural (~4-5% in US)
Discouraged workers and underemployment (U-6 measure)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing current BLS employment data: calculating rates, identifying trends, and discussing labor market implications for business

review.value: The unemployment rate when the economy is at full employment; some frictional and structural unemployment always exists even in a healthy economy
Broader measure including marginally attached workers and those working part-time for economic reasons; typically 3-5 percentage points above the standard rate

class_schedule.topic: Money, Banking, and the Federal Reserve
learning_objectives: Explain the money creation process through fractional reserve banking
Describe the Federal Reserve's structure and tools
Calculate the money multiplier and its effect on money supply
readings: Federal Reserve Structure and Functions Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Money and the banking system are the circulatory system of the economy. The Federal Reserve's decisions on monetary policy affect interest rates, investment, employment, and inflation.
key_concepts: Functions of money: medium of exchange, unit of account, store of value
Fractional reserve banking: banks lend a portion of deposits
Money multiplier = 1 / reserve requirement ratio
Federal Reserve tools: open market operations, discount rate, reserve requirements, interest on reserves
Federal funds rate and its influence on the economy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Money creation simulation: tracking how a $1,000 deposit creates money through the banking system with a 10% reserve requirement

review.value: With 10% reserve requirement, $1,000 in new deposits can create up to $10,000 in total money supply through repeated lending and redepositing
The rate banks charge each other for overnight lending; the Fed's primary tool for influencing economic activity through interest rates

learning_objectives: Construct and interpret the AD-AS model
Analyze short-run and long-run macroeconomic equilibrium
Predict effects of demand and supply shocks

lecture_notes.introduction: The AD-AS model is the core framework of macroeconomics, analogous to supply and demand in micro. It shows how the overall price level and total output are determined.
key_concepts: Aggregate demand: inverse relationship between price level and output
AD shifters: consumption, investment, government spending, net exports
Short-run AS: upward sloping (sticky wages and prices)
Long-run AS: vertical at potential GDP
Recessionary and inflationary gaps
lecture_notes.demonstration: AD-AS analysis of the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic: identifying the shocks and policy responses

review.value: When actual GDP < potential GDP; unemployment is above natural rate. AD has shifted left or SRAS has shifted left. Policy response: stimulate AD.
In the long run, wages and prices adjust, and the economy returns to potential GDP. The debate is whether to wait for adjustment or use policy to speed it up.

learning_objectives: Analyze the tools of fiscal policy: government spending and taxation
Calculate the spending multiplier and tax multiplier
Evaluate the effectiveness and limitations of fiscal policy

lecture_notes.introduction: Fiscal policy uses government spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand. Understanding the multiplier effect and its limitations is essential for evaluating policy debates.
key_concepts: Spending multiplier = 1 / (1 - MPC)
Tax multiplier = -MPC / (1 - MPC)
Automatic stabilizers: unemployment insurance, progressive taxes
Discretionary fiscal policy: timing and implementation lags
Crowding out: government borrowing raises interest rates, reducing private investment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multiplier analysis: calculating the GDP impact of a $100 billion stimulus package and a $100 billion tax cut, comparing their effectiveness

review.value: If MPC = 0.8, multiplier = 1/(1-0.8) = 5. Each $1 of government spending increases GDP by $5 through successive rounds of spending.
Government borrowing to finance spending can raise interest rates, reducing interest-sensitive private spending, partially offsetting the fiscal stimulus

learning_objectives: Explain how the Fed conducts monetary policy
Analyze the relationship between money supply, interest rates, and inflation
Evaluate the Phillips Curve and the inflation-unemployment tradeoff

lecture_notes.introduction: Monetary policy is the Fed's primary tool for managing economic fluctuations. Understanding how interest rate decisions affect the economy is critical for business planning.
key_concepts: Expansionary policy: lower rates to stimulate spending and investment
Contractionary policy: raise rates to slow inflation
Transmission mechanism: rates -> investment and consumption -> AD -> output and prices
Phillips Curve: short-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment
Taylor Rule: systematic approach to setting the federal funds rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Fed simulation exercise: acting as the FOMC, reviewing economic data and making interest rate decisions with policy justification

review.value: Short-run inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment; long-run is vertical at natural rate (no permanent tradeoff). Expectations matter.
Federal funds rate = inflation + 2% + 0.5(inflation - 2%) + 0.5(GDP gap); provides a systematic guideline for monetary policy decisions

questions.question: Country X has nominal GDP of $5 trillion in 2024 and real GDP of $4.5 trillion (base year 2020). Calculate the GDP deflator and interpret what it means.
questions.answer: GDP deflator = (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) x 100 = ($5T / $4.5T) x 100 = 111.1. This means the overall price level has risen 11.1% since the base year (2020). The gap between nominal and real GDP is entirely due to inflation; real output grew less than the nominal figures suggest.

rubric.key: Explains relationship between nominal and real

questions.question: If the reserve requirement is 8% and a new deposit of $50,000 enters the banking system, what is the maximum potential increase in the money supply? What real-world factors would make the actual increase smaller?
questions.answer: Money multiplier = 1/0.08 = 12.5. Maximum money supply increase = $50,000 x 12.5 = $625,000. Real-world factors reducing the actual increase: (1) Banks may hold excess reserves beyond the required minimum, especially during uncertain times. (2) Some money leaks out of the banking system as cash held by the public. (3) Borrowers may not spend all loan proceeds. (4) Banks may not find creditworthy borrowers for all loanable funds. The actual multiplier is typically much lower than the theoretical maximum.

rubric.key: At least two real-world limiting factors
Notes actual multiplier is lower than theoretical

questions.question: Using the AD-AS model, illustrate and explain the effects of a simultaneous positive demand shock (consumer confidence surge) and negative supply shock (oil price spike). What happens to output and price level?
questions.answer: The demand shock shifts AD right (higher output, higher prices). The supply shock shifts SRAS left (lower output, higher prices). Combined effect: the price level unambiguously rises (both shifts push prices up). The effect on output is ambiguous: AD pushes output up while SRAS pushes it down. If the demand shock dominates, output rises with higher inflation. If the supply shock dominates, output falls with even higher inflation (stagflation). The net effect on output depends on the relative magnitude of each shock.

rubric.key: Correct AD and SRAS shift directions
Output effect is ambiguous with reasoning

questions.question: With an MPC of 0.75, calculate the spending multiplier. If the government increases spending by $200 billion, what is the theoretical maximum GDP increase? Explain why the actual impact is likely smaller.
questions.answer: Spending multiplier = 1/(1-0.75) = 4. Maximum GDP increase = $200B x 4 = $800 billion. The actual impact is likely smaller because: (1) Crowding out: government borrowing raises interest rates, reducing private investment and consumption. (2) Time lags: it takes time for spending to flow through the economy. (3) Leakages: some spending goes to imports (doesn't multiply domestically) or savings. (4) Inflationary effects: if the economy is near capacity, some of the stimulus raises prices rather than real output. (5) Rational expectations: if people expect future taxes to pay for spending, they may save more now (Ricardian equivalence).

rubric.key: At least two reasons actual impact is smaller

major_assignments.title: Macroeconomic Analysis and Policy Brief
major_assignments.description: Analyze the current macroeconomic environment of a country of your choice, including GDP growth, unemployment, inflation, and monetary/fiscal policy. Produce a professional policy brief recommending economic policy actions.
objectives: Collect and interpret macroeconomic data from official sources
Apply AD-AS analysis to the current economic situation
Evaluate current monetary and fiscal policy effectiveness
Recommend policy actions based on economic analysis
deliverables: Macroeconomic data summary with trend analysis (3 years)
AD-AS model application to current economic conditions
Data appendix with all sources cited

criteria: Key indicators (GDP, unemployment, inflation, rates) collected
Data from credible sources (BEA, BLS, Fed, IMF)
AD-AS model correctly applied to current conditions
Monetary and fiscal policy effects analyzed
Supported by theoretical and empirical evidence
Correct application of AD-AS and policy frameworks
Specific policy recommendations with tradeoff analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of macroeconomic concepts and graphical analysis

problem_sets.description: Weekly problems covering GDP, money, AD-AS, and policy analysis

midterm_examination.description: Covers GDP, unemployment, money, and banking

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including AD-AS, fiscal, and monetary policy

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Current events discussions at start of each class require preparation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 10% per day. Policy brief has firm deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem sets may be discussed but must be submitted independently. Policy brief is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All data and analysis must be original. Citing economist opinions requires proper attribution.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to FRED, BLS, and BEA data. Graphing calculator or software. Excel for data analysis.
